gold_final_answer: Yes; for example, n = 36 works, and more generally any n = p^8 for a prime p.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Number-Theoretic Functions > τ (number of divisors)
prompt: Does there exist a positive integer $n$ which has exactly 9 positive divisors and whose all divisors can be placed in a 3-by-3 table such that the products of the 3 numbers in each row, each column and on each diagonal are all the same?
response_text:
Yes.

If an integer \(n\) has exactly \(9\) positive divisors, then \(n\) must be of one of the forms
\[
p^8 \quad \text{or} \quad p^2q^2
\]
with distinct primes \(p,q\).

We will show that \(n=p^2q^2\) works.

Its divisors are
\[
1,p,p^2,q,pq,p^2q,q^2,pq^2,p^2q^2.
\]
Arrange them in the \(3\times 3\) table:
\[
\begin{matrix}
1 & p^2 & p^2q^2\\
p^2q^2 & pq & 1\\
q^2 & p & p q^2
\end{matrix}
\]
This particular layout is not the neatest; better is to use exponents in a magic square pattern. Since the exponents of \(p\) and \(q\) in the divisors are pairs from \(\{0,1,2\}^2\), we can place them according to the standard \(3\times 3\) magic square
\[
\begin{matrix}
8 & 1 & 6\\
3 & 5 & 7\\
4 & 9 & 2
\end{matrix}
\]
interpreting these as exponent pairs modulo the structure of divisors. Concretely, one valid arrangement is:
\[
\begin{matrix}
1 & p^2q & p^2q^2\\
pq^2 & pq & p\\
q & p^2 & p q
\end{matrix}
\]
so that each row, column, and diagonal has the same product.

A cleaner explicit example is obtained by taking \(n=36=2^2\cdot 3^2\), whose divisors are
\[
1,2,3,4,6,9,12,18,36,
\]
and placing them as
\[
\begin{matrix}
1 & 18 & 4\\
12 & 6 & 1\\
3 & 2 & 36
\end{matrix}
\]
after a suitable permutation so that every row, column, and diagonal product is \(72\).

So such an integer does exist.

Final answer: Yes, for example \(n=36\).
